common.pageTitle: Pickssue - Personalized Open Source Contribution Curator
common.description: A personalized open-source contribution curator that analyzes your GitHub activity to recommend the best issues for you
common.loginRequired: Please log in with GitHub to use this feature.
common.loginToManage: Log in to track repositories, customize settings, and get notifications.

recommended.title: Recommended Issues

footer.description: Helping developers discover beginner-friendly open source issues

repositoriesGuest.trackingDesc1: Repository Tracking is the core feature of Pickssue. Once you sign in with GitHub, you can add any public GitHub repository to your personal tracking list. The app continuously monitors those repositories and surfaces open issues labeled as beginner-friendly — so you never have to manually browse dozens of issue trackers.
repositoriesGuest.trackingDesc2: You can track as many repositories as you like. Each repository is shown as a card with a direct link to its GitHub page. Removing a repository from your list takes a single click and never affects the upstream project in any way.
repositoriesGuest.featuresTitle: Everything You Can Do After Signing In

features.addRepos: Add Repositories by URL or owner/name
features.addReposDesc: Paste a full GitHub URL like https://github.com/facebook/react or type the short form facebook/react — the app resolves it automatically.
features.monitorIssues: Monitor Beginner-Friendly Issues in Real Time
features.monitorIssuesDesc: Issues are fetched directly from the GitHub API and refreshed on demand. Filter by label, sort by date or comments, and hide issues that are already closed.
features.removeReposDesc: Keep your list focused. Remove repositories you no longer want to track with a single click — your other tracked repos are unaffected.
features.syncSettings: Sync Settings Across Devices via Cloud
features.syncSettingsDesc: Your tracked repository list is saved to the cloud. Sign in from any device and your entire list is restored automatically.
features.notifications: Get Browser Notifications for New Issues
features.notificationsDesc: Enable desktop notifications in Settings and be alerted whenever a new beginner-friendly issue appears in one of your tracked repositories.

repositoriesGuest.popularTitle: Popular Beginner-Friendly Repositories to Get Started
repositoriesGuest.popularDesc: Not sure where to begin? These well-maintained open-source projects have active maintainers and regularly label issues for new contributors.

steps.step1Desc: OAuth login grants read access to public repositories. Settings are synced via cloud storage. No write access to your code is ever requested.
steps.step2Desc: Enter a GitHub repository URL or the short owner/name format. The app validates that the repository exists before adding it.
steps.step3Desc: Switch to the Issues tab to see all beginner-friendly issues across your tracked repositories, sorted by newest first.
steps.step4Desc: Click any issue to open it on GitHub. Read the discussion, ask questions, and submit your first pull request.

settingsGuest.customizeDesc1: The Settings page lets you tailor every aspect of how Pickssue works for you. Once you sign in with GitHub you can configure notification frequency, define custom issue-label filters, decide whether to show or hide closed issues, and manage cross-device synchronization — all from a single panel.
settingsGuest.customizeDesc2: Every setting is persisted in your browser and optionally backed up to the cloud so your preferences follow you across devices and browsers. Changes take effect immediately — no page reload required.
settingsGuest.notificationFrequencyDesc: Choose how often the app checks for new beginner-friendly issues — every 15 minutes, 30 minutes, hourly, or on-demand. Browser notifications are delivered instantly when a matching issue appears so you can act on it before others.
settingsGuest.customLabelFilteringDesc: Beyond the built-in good first issue and help wanted filters, you can add any GitHub label you care about. Toggle individual labels on or off to show only the issue types that match your skills and interests.
settingsGuest.hideClosedIssuesDesc: GitHub occasionally keeps closed issues visible for historical reference. Toggle this option on to keep your feed clean and focused on actionable, open issues only — reducing noise when you are scanning for your next contribution opportunity.
settingsGuest.gistSyncDesc: Save your entire settings — tracked repositories, label filters, and preferences — to the cloud with one click. Load them back at any time or on any device. Conflict resolution is built in, so you always stay in control of which version wins.
settingsGuest.crossDeviceDesc1: Pickssue uses cloud storage to sync your settings across devices — no separate account or third-party service required. When you trigger a sync, the app serializes your current settings and saves them securely so only you can read them.
settingsGuest.crossDeviceDesc2: On another device, sign in with the same GitHub account and load your settings to restore everything instantly. If the remote settings and your local settings have diverged, a conflict resolution dialog guides you through choosing which version to keep — local, remote, or a manual merge.

syncSteps.step1Desc: Click "Save to Cloud" to upload your current settings to secure cloud storage.
syncSteps.step2Desc: On any device, sign in with your GitHub account to access the sync feature.
syncSteps.step3Desc: Click "Load from Cloud" to restore your tracked repositories and preferences.

settingsGuest.whyTitle: Why These Settings Matter for Open-Source Contributors
settingsGuest.whyDesc1: Finding the right issue to work on is one of the biggest hurdles for new open-source contributors. The default GitHub interface shows every issue across a repository — bugs, feature requests, performance work, and refactors — without distinguishing which ones are suitable for beginners. Pickssue solves this by analyzing your GitHub activity and recommending issues that match your skills and interests.
settingsGuest.whyDesc2: Notification frequency matters too. Many beginner-friendly issues are claimed within hours of being labeled. Setting a shorter polling interval means you hear about new issues faster, giving you a better chance of being the first to comment and claim the work. Combined with custom label filters, you can build a highly targeted feed that surfaces exactly the kind of issues you are ready to tackle.
settingsGuest.whyDesc3: All settings are stored locally in your browser first, so the app works even without an internet connection after the initial load. The cloud sync layer is purely optional and additive — it adds persistence across devices without removing local control.

picked.title: Picked Issues
picked.guestTitle: Save Issues You Want to Work On
picked.guestDesc: Sign in with GitHub to pick interesting issues from Recommended Issues. Track their status and get notified when they change.
picked.emptyDesc: Browse Recommended Issues and pick the ones you're interested in.
picked.contributionVerifiedTooltip: Your pull request closed this issue

languageNav.heading: Language-Specific Contribution Guides
languageNav.description: Want a guide tailored to your language? Each page covers recommended repositories, issue types to target, and language-specific tips for your first contribution.

python.intro: Readable, versatile, and backed by one of the friendliest open source communities.

go.intro: Simple, fast, and built for production — with a toolchain that makes contributing a breeze.

rust.intro: Systems programming with memory safety — and a community known for exceptional documentation.

java.title: Java Open Source Contribution Guide for Beginners
java.intro: The backbone of enterprise software — with a vast ecosystem and decades of beginner-friendly projects.
